title: Why Were Prehistoric Insects So Huge? New Study Flips Old Theory
summary:
Three hundred million years ago, Earth was a very different place. The continents were mashed into a supercontinent called Pangaea, and giant coal-swamp forests covered the land. The air had much more oxygen than today, and the skies were ruled by enormous insects. Dragonfly-like creatures called griffinflies had wingspans of up to 27 inches (70 cm)—about the size of a modern seagull. For decades, scientists thought these giants existed because high oxygen levels allowed them to grow so large. But a new study published in Nature flips that idea on its head.

Insects don't breathe with lungs. Instead, they use a network of tiny tubes called a tracheal system. Oxygen travels through these tubes to reach muscles. The old theory said that bigger insects needed more oxygen, and that higher atmospheric oxygen made their size possible. But researchers led by Edward Snelling from the University of Pretoria used high-power electron microscopes to examine insect flight muscles. They discovered that the oxygen-delivering tubes, called tracheoles, take up only about 1% of the flight muscle—even in the largest insects. That's a tiny fraction.

"If atmospheric oxygen really set a limit on insect size, we'd expect to see more tracheoles in bigger insects," Snelling explained. "But there's almost no difference." In fact, birds and mammals have capillaries that take up ten times more space in their heart muscles than tracheoles do in insect flight muscle. That suggests insects could easily add more tracheoles if oxygen were the problem.

So what did make prehistoric insects so huge? The mystery remains. Some scientists think oxygen might still play a role in other parts of the body, but for now, the old theory is on shaky ground. Other possibilities include escaping predators or physical limits of their exoskeleton. The search for answers continues, and it's an exciting puzzle for scientists to solve.
why_it_matters: Understanding how body size evolves helps scientists grasp the limits of life on Earth—and could inform how creatures might adapt to future changes in oxygen or climate.

keywords.explanation: A single vast landmass made up of many modern continents joined together.
The name of the supercontinent that existed about 300 million years ago.
A network of tiny tubes that insects use to breathe instead of lungs.
The smallest tubes in the insect breathing system that deliver oxygen directly to muscles.
Very thin blood vessels in birds and mammals that carry oxygen to heart muscle.
A hard outer shell that supports and protects an insect's body.

options: Higher oxygen levels in the atmosphere

questions.correct_answer: Through a network of tiny tubes called a tracheal system

It was because of low carbon dioxide levels

questions.correct_answer: The mystery remains, and other factors like escaping predators are being considered

background_read: The Carboniferous Period (about 359 to 299 million years ago) is known for vast swamp forests and extremely high atmospheric oxygen—up to 35% compared to today's 21%. This led to the 'oxygen hypothesis' for giant insects, which held sway for decades.

Edward Snelling at the University of Pretoria (a leading research university in South Africa) and his team used electron microscopes to examine flight muscles of modern insects. Their discovery that tracheoles occupy barely 1% of muscle volume challenges the oxygen limit theory.

In birds and mammals, capillaries take up ten times more space in heart muscles than insect tracheoles do in flight muscles, suggesting insects have unused capacity to deliver oxygen. This opens new questions about what truly limited their size—possibly predators, exoskeleton mechanics, or other factors.
Article_Structure: LEAD: old theory that high oxygen made prehistoric insects huge
└─ specific: griffinflies with 27-inch wingspan, 300 million years ago
KEY EVENT: new study in Nature flips that idea
├─ WHO: Edward Snelling, University of Pretoria
├─ HOW: high-power electron microscopes of insect flight muscles
└─ FINDING: tracheoles only 1% of flight muscle, no difference in bigger insects
EVIDENCE: birds/mammals have 10x more space in heart muscles for capillaries
OPEN QUESTION: what really made insects huge? Predators? Exoskeleton limits?
IMPLICATIONS: old theory on shaky ground, puzzle remains

perspectives.description: The study overturns a long-held assumption and opens new avenues for research. It shows how new technology (electron microscopes) can challenge old ideas, advancing our understanding of evolutionary biology.

The old theory is debunked but no clear replacement is offered, leaving a gap in knowledge. Some may argue the study only focused on flight muscles, and oxygen might still limit size in other tissues or growth stages.

The research is based on modern insects, not fossils. It assumes tracheole proportion is similar in ancient insects, which may not be true. The article highlights that correlation (high oxygen + big insects) doesn't prove causation.

Researchers will now explore other hypotheses, like predator pressure or exoskeleton physics. This could lead to a more complete picture of insect evolution and the role of environmental factors in shaping life's history.
